{"step_by_step_thinking": "Tregs (CD4+CD25+ regulatory T cells) are defined as cells that modulate immune responses and inhibit autoimmune processes. Multiple documents confirm that T regulatory cells suppress immune responses by producing inhibitory cytokines such as IL-10 and IL-35, using CTLA-4 to inhibit B7 on antigen-presenting cells, and by maintaining immune tolerance. For example, Document [0] states that T regulatory cells'modulate immune responses and inhibit autoimmune processes' and 'produce inhibitory IL-10 and IL-35 cytokines to control immune responses'. Document [16] explicitly states that regulatory T cells'suppress immune responses, maintain tolerance to self-antigens, and prevent autoimmune diseases'. Document [29] notes that immunotherapy induces 'FOXP3+ CD25+ regulatory T cells' that provide 'allergenic tolerance' and 'induce regulatory cytokines' that skew the response from TH2 to Treg. These mechanisms indicate that Tregs function as negative regulators, not positive regulators, of the immune response. A positive regulator would enhance immune activity, whereas Tregs dampen it. Therefore, Tregs are not positive regulators but rather suppressors.", "answer_choice": "B"}
